summary: It's been a wild Monday in Atlanta with Reynoldstown finally seeing movement on a stalled BeltLine project, while ICE agents showed up at the airport to help with massive TSA lines caused by the government shutdown. Plus, Inman Park just got a new Asian-American restaurant that's already turning heads.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Stalled BeltLine Corner Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After sitting empty for nearly five years, that cleared corner at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace is finally seeing action. Embry Development is moving forward with 85 residential units just a block from the Eastside Trail, taking over what Toll Brothers started but never finished.
topStory.body:
Remember that sad, empty lot on Chester Avenue near the BeltLine that's been sitting there since they cleared the buildings back in 2021? Well, grab your hard hat because things are finally happening. Embry Development has taken over from Toll Brothers and started construction on what they're calling Camber Crossing, an 85-unit mix of townhomes and condos about a block west of the Eastside Trail.

This corner at Chester and Fulton Terrace has been a neighborhood eyesore for way too long, especially considering how hot Reynoldstown real estate has become. The original developer got the permits but never followed through, leaving residents to wonder if that prime spot near Memorial Drive would stay vacant forever. Now locals can finally see crews working the site, which should bring some much-needed housing to an area that's been gentrifying rapidly.

Embry Development's managing partner says they're aiming to create something that fits the neighborhood's character while adding density near transit. Given how quickly things sell in Reynoldstown these days, especially anything within walking distance of the BeltLine, these units will probably move fast once they hit the market.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment at Camber Crossing development site in Reynoldstown

stories.imageAlt: Construction equipment at Camber Crossing development site in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Decatur Gets 'Missing Middle' Housing at Cottages at Midway
stories.summary: A village-style development on Midway Road is trying to crack the affordability code with 18 standalone houses around shared courtyards. With Decatur home prices hitting $700K, this mixed-income project could be a model for accessible ITP living.
stories.body:
You know how everyone complains about the lack of affordable housing options ITP, especially in Decatur where the median home price just crossed $700,000? Well, Cottages at Midway on Midway Road might be part of the solution. This 2.1-acre project is bringing 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards and green space, specifically designed as 'missing middle' housing that falls between apartments and expensive single-family homes.

What makes this interesting is the mixed-income approach. Instead of building another luxury development that only tech workers can afford, the developers are creating a range of price points that could actually work for teachers, firefighters, and other folks who keep the community running but can't drop three-quarters of a million on a house.

The soft launch starts next week, and if this model works, it could be replicated elsewhere around the metro. The location near major commercial corridors and downtown Decatur makes it even more appealing for people who want that ITP lifestyle without the ITP mortgage payment. Given how quickly things move in Decatur's real estate market, interested buyers should probably pay attention sooner rather than later.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan for Cottages at Midway development showing house layout

stories.headline: West End's BeltLine Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is finally rising from that cleared lot at 600 Hopkins Street. Ackerman & Co. is building 23 for-sale townhomes near Lee + White, with the first units hitting the market this spring.
stories.body:
If you've been walking the Westside Trail and wondering what's happening with that construction site at 600 Hopkins Street, here's your answer: GW Parkside is finally taking shape. Ackerman & Co. (the same folks behind Lee + White) started demolition a year ago and now the first seven townhomes are nearing completion, with marketing materials promising the first units will be available this spring.

This project is significant because for-sale townhomes are pretty rare along the Southwest Atlanta portion of the BeltLine, where most new construction has been rentals. The 23-unit development sits in a sweet spot near the expanding Lee + White complex, which has become a legitimate dining and office destination over the past few years.

The West End has been slower to gentrify compared to the eastside BeltLine neighborhoods, but projects like this suggest that's changing. With the renamed GW Parkside, Ackerman is clearly betting that people want to own (not just rent) near the trail on this side of town. Given the success of Lee + White as an anchor, they're probably right.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes development in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. Expect Peking chicken, soft shell crab bao, and Thai tea martinis in a space that started as a pop-up and grew into something special.
stories.body:
You know that spot on Elizabeth Street where all the good Inman Park restaurants seem to cluster? Well, add Mushi Ni to the list. Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez officially opened their Asian-American comfort food concept today, and based on their pop-up success, this one's going to be busy from day one.

Le and Jimenez aren't newcomers to the Atlanta food scene. She worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving here, and they've both been grinding in local kitchens for years before deciding to do their own thing. The menu hits all the comfort food notes: soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, crab Rangoon puffs, and their signature Peking chicken platter that feeds two people for $25.

The location is perfect for both lunch and dinner, especially with its proximity to the Freedom Park Trail. And honestly, Inman Park needed another solid Asian option beyond the usual suspects. If their cocktail program (hello, Thai tea martinis) lives up to the food, this could become one of those neighborhood spots that people from other parts of town start making the trip for.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish from Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's Eastside Food Hall Sets April Opening
stories.summary: The Gansevoort Market creator is bringing his third Atlanta food concept to the new Vela Park development near East Lake Golf Club. Six chef-driven concepts plus craft cocktails are coming to this 12-acre rental complex in mid-April.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid knows how to create food destinations. After launching Gansevoort Market in New York and establishing himself in Atlanta's food scene, he's now partnering with the developers of Vela Park (that new rental complex near East Lake Golf Club) to open what could be the eastside's next big food hall.

Vela Park Food Works is wrapping up construction with six different chef-driven concepts under one roof, plus a full craft cocktail program and plenty of outdoor dining space. The mid-April opening puts it right in time for spring weather, which should be perfect for that outdoor seating they're planning.

What makes this particularly interesting is the location. East Lake has been quietly developing over the past few years, and a serious food hall could be the amenity that really puts this area on the map for people who don't live in the immediate neighborhood. Given Montwaid's track record with creating food destinations that become community gathering spots, Vela Park Food Works could end up being more than just another place to grab lunch.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works food hall

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style BBQ to the BeltLine
stories.summary: Pitmaster John Lewis has opened his smokehouse at Ansley Mall behind The Cook's Warehouse, complete with a panoramic loft where six 22-foot smokers run 24/7. The view of the BeltLine pedestrian bridge might be as impressive as the Texas-style brisket.
stories.body:
John Lewis didn't just open another barbecue joint. He built what might be the most spectacular smokehouse in Georgia, with six massive 22-foot smokers housed in a panoramic loft overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. The setup at Ansley Mall looks more like a fancy engine room than your typical BBQ kitchen, and the brisket coming out of those custom-welded smokers is worth the visual spectacle.

Lewis helped design and weld those smokers himself, turning 1,000-gallon propane tanks into precision cooking machines that run in staggered eight-hour shifts around the clock. The Austin-by-way-of-Charleston influence shows in the final product, with Texas-style technique producing some of the best brisket you'll find in Atlanta.

The location is smart too. Ansley Mall has been quietly becoming a food destination, and the proximity to the BeltLine means both locals and trail users can easily grab some seriously good barbecue. Plus, watching those smokers work through those panoramic windows adds dinner theater to the whole experience.

stories.headline: ICE Agents Show Up at Atlanta Airport as TSA Lines Stretch for Hours
stories.summary: Federal Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents are now walking around Hartsfield-Jackson to help with security as the government shutdown leaves TSA agents working without pay. Mayor Dickens assured everyone they won't do immigration enforcement, but travelers say they're mostly just standing around.
stories.body:
If you've been to the airport lately, you've probably noticed the extra federal agents in tactical gear walking around. ICE agents started showing up Monday to help with the chaos caused by TSA agents calling out sick in record numbers during the partial government shutdown. About 41% of Atlanta's TSA workforce didn't show up for work over the weekend, creating those legendary four-hour wait times we've all been hearing about.

Mayor Andre Dickens was quick to assure everyone that these ICE agents won't be doing immigration enforcement while they're at the airport. They're supposed to provide general security support to help TSA operations, though travelers are telling reporters the agents seem to be mostly standing around without clear instructions on what they're actually supposed to do.

The whole situation highlights just how bad things have gotten at Hartsfield-Jackson. When you need to bring in immigration agents to help people get through security lines, you know the government shutdown is hitting home in a very real way. Delta even suspended its special services for members of Congress, basically telling lawmakers to wait in line like everyone else until they figure out how to fund TSA properly.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Project Nearly Done on West Howard
stories.summary: That bike lane project on West Howard Avenue between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA is almost finished. The two-way cycle track with concrete barriers should make the corridor much more bike and pedestrian friendly after years of being a car speedway.
stories.body:
After nearly a decade of planning, Decatur's transformation of West Howard Avenue from a high-speed car corridor into a proper Complete Street is almost done. The project runs about a mile from Commerce Drive near Decatur High School all the way to the city limits at Paden Circle by East Lake MARTA station, and the difference is dramatic.

The big addition is a two-way cycle track protected by concrete barriers, which should make bike commuting between downtown Decatur and the East Lake area much safer. Anyone who's tried to bike or walk along West Howard before knows it felt like taking your life in your hands with cars flying by at highway speeds.

Work started back in August, and the timing couldn't be better with spring weather making people want to get outside and use alternative transportation. The connection to East Lake MARTA is particularly smart, giving people a car-free way to get from transit to Decatur's downtown core. This is the kind of infrastructure investment that actually changes how people move around the city.
stories.imageAlt: New bike lane infrastructure on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: City Council Moves Forward with ICE Detention Ban
stories.summary: Atlanta's Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced resolutions to bar ICE detention facilities in city limits and require documentation when ICE agents are present during police operations. The full council votes April 20th.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council is taking a clear stance on immigration enforcement within city limits. The Public Safety Committee unanimously passed two resolutions Monday that would ban ICE detention facilities in the city and require Atlanta police to document any time ICE agents are present during their operations. Both measures head to the full council for a vote on April 20.

The timing is particularly pointed given that ICE agents are currently deployed at Hartsfield-Jackson to help with airport security during the government shutdown. While Mayor Dickens has assured residents these agents won't do immigration enforcement at the airport, council members clearly want formal protections in place.

Firefighters also showed up at the meeting to criticize Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign their first-ever union contract, which the council already ratified back in April 2025. The disconnect between the mayor and council on labor issues is becoming a recurring theme, and it's creating some awkward political dynamics as we head into budget season.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting in session

stories.headline: Atlantic Station and Midtown Land in National Top 10
stories.summary: Niche.com ranked Atlantic Station as the #1 best place to live in America, with Midtown coming in at #6. The rankings looked at schools, cost of living, jobs, crime, and amenities across more than 18,000 neighborhoods nationwide.
stories.body:
Atlanta just got some serious national recognition, and it might surprise you which neighborhoods made the cut. Atlantic Station landed the #1 spot on Niche.com's 'Best Places to Live in America' list, with Midtown not far behind at #6. That puts Atlanta neighborhoods in 20% of the national top 10, which is pretty impressive for any metro area.

The rankings considered public schools, cost of living, job opportunities, crime rates, and local amenities across more than 18,000 neighborhoods nationwide. Atlantic Station's win likely reflects its walkability, proximity to Midtown jobs, and the mix of retail and dining options that have developed over the past few years.

Midtown's #6 ranking makes sense too, given its central location, MARTA access, cultural attractions, and job density. Both neighborhoods offer that urban lifestyle without some of the drawbacks you might find in other major cities. Of course, anyone trying to rent or buy in either area might have some thoughts about that 'cost of living' factor in the rankings.
stories.imageAlt: Aerial view of Atlantic Station development
